infobox.value: (November 25, 1857 – January 30, 1942) was a lawyer and a long-serving Canadian politician and judge. His career in legislatures of the North-West Territories and Saskatchewan stretched into four decades. He served as the first [premier of the North-West Territories](/wiki/Premier_of_the_North-West_Territories "Premier of the North-West Territories") from 1897 to 1905 and is recognized as having a significant contribution towards the creation of the provinces of [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"). From 1905 on he served as Leader of the Official Opposition in Saskatchewan as well as leader of the Provincial Rights Party. His legislative career ended when he was appointed to the judiciary in 1912.

**Sir Frederick William Alpin Gordon Haultain** (November 25, 1857 – January 30, 1942) was a lawyer and a long-serving Canadian politician and judge. His career in legislatures of the North-West Territories and Saskatchewan stretched into four decades. He served as the first [premier of the North-West Territories](/wiki/Premier_of_the_North-West_Territories "Premier of the North-West Territories") from 1897 to 1905 and is recognized as having a significant contribution towards the creation of the provinces of [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"). From 1905 on he served as Leader of the Official Opposition in Saskatchewan as well as leader of the Provincial Rights Party. His legislative career ended when he was appointed to the judiciary in 1912.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frederick_W._A._G._Haultain&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

He was born in [Woolwich](/wiki/Woolwich "Woolwich"), England in 1857, the son of [Frederick W. Haultain](/wiki/Frederick_W._Haultain "Frederick W. Haultain") (1821–1882) and Lucinde Helen Gordon (1828–1915),[[1]](#cite_note-1) and came to [Peterborough, Canada West](/wiki/Peterborough,_Ontario "Peterborough, Ontario"), with his family in 1860. He grew up in Peterborough and [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), where he was educated at the [High School of Montreal](/wiki/High_School_of_Montreal "High School of Montreal"), later receiving a [Bachelor of Arts](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") from the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"). He later studied law at [Osgoode Hall](/wiki/Osgoode_Hall "Osgoode Hall") and was called to the bar in Ontario in 1882 and in the North-West Territories in 1884.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## North-West territorial politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frederick_W._A._G._Haultain&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: North-West territorial politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Frederick_W.G._Haultain_na-510-1.jpg/250px-Frederick_W.G._Haultain_na-510-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frederick_W.G._Haultain_na-510-1.jpg)

Haultain in 1884

In 1884, Haultain opened a law practice in [Fort Macleod](/wiki/Fort_Macleod "Fort Macleod"). He also served as Crown prosecutor there for several years, and also was an editor for newspapers in Fort Macleod and [Lethbridge](/wiki/Lethbridge "Lethbridge"). Haultain was elected to the [Legislative Assembly of the North-West Territories](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_the_North-West_Territories "Legislative Assembly of the North-West Territories") in a by-election held on September 5, 1887. He defeated [Charles Conybeare](/wiki/Charles_F._P._Conybeare "Charles F. P. Conybeare") by a large margin. Representing the electoral district of Macleod in the North-West Territorial Council from 1887 to 1888. Haultain won his next five elections by [acclamation](/wiki/Acclamation "Acclamation").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The editor of the Calgary Herald once wrote of him saying that "He is a man of academic training and large, clear perception; straightforward and manly even towards his enemies. In some respects, he is the most finished debater ever heard on a Western platform, arraying his Facts in crisp, clear-cut sentences, and then pressing home his argument with logic and Force?"[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Premier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frederick_W._A._G._Haultain&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Premier")]

Haultain was appointed premier of the North-West Territories on October 7, 1897. Haultain also served as Attorney General and Commissioner of Education.[[5]](#cite_note-5) As premier, Haultain led negotiations for the granting of provincial status. He argued for Alberta and Saskatchewan to be admitted as a single province named [Buffalo](/wiki/Province_of_Buffalo "Province of Buffalo"),[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpencer20074-6) and wanted the new province to be governed by non-partisan governments. The federal [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") government of Sir [Wilfrid Laurier](/wiki/Wilfrid_Laurier "Wilfrid Laurier"), however, decided against such a large province and favoured creating two provinces, Alberta and Saskatchewan, which eventually happened in 1905.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Frederick_WAG_Haultain.jpg/250px-Frederick_WAG_Haultain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frederick_WAG_Haultain.jpg)

Haultain in 1941

Frustrated in negotiations with the federal Liberal government, Haultain increasingly identified with the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada_(historical) "Conservative Party of Canada (historical)") and campaigned for it in the [1904 federal election](/wiki/1904_Canadian_federal_election "1904 Canadian federal election"). Laurier's Liberals were re-elected to a majority of seats in the House of Commons, and took two seats in the Alberta district, Conservative party candidates taking the other two.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Saskatchewan politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frederick_W._A._G._Haultain&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Saskatchewan politics")]

After Alberta and Saskatchewan became provinces in 1905, Haultain chose to be active in Saskatchewan. He led the Saskatchewan [Provincial Rights Party](/wiki/Provincial_Rights_Party "Provincial Rights Party") in the [1905 Saskatchewan provincial election](/wiki/1905_Saskatchewan_general_election "1905 Saskatchewan general election"), which was won by the [Liberal Party of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Liberal Party of Saskatchewan"). From 1905 to 1912, Haultain sat in the [Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") as [leader of the Opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Opposition_(Saskatchewan) "Leader of the Opposition (Saskatchewan)").

## Later life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frederick_W._A._G._Haultain&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Later life")]

In 1912, the newly elected Conservative federal government of Sir [Robert Borden](/wiki/Robert_Borden "Robert Borden") made Haultain [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice "Chief Justice") of Saskatchewan's [superior court](/wiki/Superior_court "Superior court"). Haultain was [knighted](/wiki/Knighted "Knighted") in 1916. The next year, he became [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice "Chief Justice") of the Saskatchewan [Court of Appeal](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_courts_of_appeal "List of Canadian courts of appeal"), a position that he held until his retirement in 1938.

Haultain died on January 30, 1942, at Montreal.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Further reading
